---
title: "Regional Inequality from Outer Space"
subtitle: "Predicting GDP from nighttime lights and building inequality indices in Python"
date: "2026-06-15"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python_kuznets_dmsp-tutorial
---

This is the runnable companion to the blog post of the same name — a replication of
**Lessmann & Seidel (2017)**, "Regional inequality, convergence, and its determinants —
A view from outer space." We predict regional GDP from satellite nighttime lights, build
five population-weighted inequality indices from scratch, and estimate the regional
Kuznets curve, its determinants, and a spatial-HAC robustness check with **PyFixest**.

The bundle builds a hermetic `.venv/` for you (see `README.md`); the chunk below confirms
the tutorial is running inside it.

```{python}
#| label: env-check
import sys
from pathlib import Path
in_venv = ".venv" in Path(sys.executable).resolve().parts
print("Running inside the bundle .venv:" , in_venv)
print("Python:", sys.version.split()[0])
```

## Setup and imports

```{python}
#| label: imports
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pyfixest as pf
import maketables as mt
from linearmodels.panel import RandomEffects
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

STEEL, ORANGE, INK, TEAL = "#6a9bcc", "#d97757", "#141413", "#00d4c8"
np.random.seed(42)
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 110, "axes.spines.top": False,
                     "axes.spines.right": False, "axes.grid": True,
                     "grid.color": "#e6e3dd"})
```

## Loading the data

The small CSVs are downloaded from the post's public GitHub folder, with a local
`data/` fallback.

```{python}
#| label: load
BASE = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/"
        "master/content/post/python_kuznets_dmsp/data/")

def load(name):
    try:
        return pd.read_csv(BASE + name)
    except Exception:
        return pd.read_csv("data/" + name)

pred = load("Prediction_Data.csv")   # region-year: lights -> GDP training set
t2   = load("Table_2_data.csv")      # region-year: inequality-index inputs
t3   = load("Table_3_data.csv")      # country-year: Kuznets data
t4   = load("Table_4_data.csv")      # country-year: determinants
tb4  = load("Table_B4_data.csv")     # region-year: lat/lon for spatial errors
f5   = load("Figure_5_data.csv")     # country-year: regional vs personal Gini
print("region-years:", pred.shape[0], " | country-years:", t3.shape[0])

GRP = {"eap": "East Asia & Pacific", "eca": "Europe & Central Asia",
       "lac": "Latin America & Carib.", "mena": "Mid. East & N. Africa",
       "sa": "South Asia", "ssa": "Sub-Saharan Africa"}
def row_group(r):
    for g in GRP:
        if r.get(g, 0) == 1:
            return GRP[g]
    return "N. America & high-inc."
pred["wb_group"] = pred.apply(row_group, axis=1)
pred["satyear"]  = sum(i * pred[f"satyear_{i}"] for i in range(1, 8)).astype(int)
pred["group_id"] = pred["wb_group"]
country_group = pred.groupby("Country_ISO")["wb_group"].agg(
    lambda s: s.value_counts().index[0])
```

### Variables, sources, and descriptive statistics

The variables come from Lessmann and Seidel (2017): nighttime lights are the DMSP-OLS
stable-lights product (NOAA/NGDC) aggregated to GADM 1st-level regions; observed regional income
is from Gennaioli et al. (2014); population from GPW v3 (CIESIN); the inequality indices are
population-weighted Gini/CV/GE built from predicted regional income; and the determinants are
mostly World Bank WDI series. The post's
[Appendix A](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/python_kuznets_dmsp/#appendix-a-data-dictionary) is a
full data dictionary — every column, with construction, source, and time–country coverage. Two
summary tables give the variables' shape (region files span 1992–2010; country files 1992–2012):

```{python}
#| label: summary-stats
def _f(v):
    if pd.isna(v):
        return "--"
    a = abs(v)
    return f"{v:,.0f}" if a >= 1000 else (f"{v:.2f}" if a >= 1 else f"{v:.4f}")

def summarise_panel(spec, y0, y1):
    fns = [("mean", "mean"), ("median", "median"), ("sd", "std"), ("min", "min"), ("max", "max")]
    rows = {}
    for label, df, col in spec:
        s = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce"); yr = df["year"]
        obs = sorted(yr[s.notna()].unique())
        def ys(t, last=False):
            v = s[yr == t]
            return v if (v.notna().any() or not obs) else s[yr == (obs[-1] if last else obs[0])]
        vi, vf = ys(y0), ys(y1, last=True)
        rows[label] = [_f(getattr(v, fn)()) for _, fn in fns for v in (vi, vf)]
    cols = pd.MultiIndex.from_tuples([(l, str(y)) for l, _ in fns for y in (y0, y1)])
    return pd.DataFrame.from_dict(rows, orient="index", columns=cols)

country = summarise_panel([
    ("GDP p.c. (country, $)", t3, "GDP_pc_Country"),
    ("Regional Gini (GINIW)", t3, "GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"),
    ("Resource rents (% GDP)", t4, "Resources_rents_share_of_GDP"),
    ("Gasoline price ($/L)", t4, "price_gasoline"),
    ("Personal income Gini", f5, "Giniall"),
], 1992, 2012)
# each statistic computed for the initial vs final panel year
mt.MTable(country).make("gt")
```

### Exploratory data analysis

Summary tables hide how the *whole distribution* moves over time. Box-plots over the five 5-year
periods (the same periods used in the Kuznets regressions) show both the cross-sectional spread (box
height) and the time dynamics (left → right) — once for region-level, once for country-level variables.

```{python}
#| label: eda-boxplots
def period_boxes(ax, df, unit, col, title, logy=False):
    d = df[df.year.between(1990, 2014)].assign(
        p=lambda x: pd.cut(x.year, [1989, 1994, 1999, 2004, 2009, 2014],
                           labels=["90-94", "95-99", "00-04", "05-09", "10-14"]))
    g = (d[["p", col]] if unit is None
         else d.groupby([unit, "p"], observed=True)[col].mean().reset_index())
    cats = [c for c in ["90-94", "95-99", "00-04", "05-09", "10-14"] if (g["p"] == c).any()]
    bp = ax.boxplot([pd.to_numeric(g.loc[g["p"] == c, col], errors="coerce").dropna() for c in cats],
                    patch_artist=True, showfliers=False, widths=0.6)
    for b in bp["boxes"]:
        b.set(facecolor=STEEL, alpha=0.65, edgecolor=INK)
    for m in bp["medians"]:
        m.set(color=ORANGE, linewidth=2)
    ax.set_xticks(range(1, len(cats) + 1)); ax.set_xticklabels(cats)
    if logy:
        ax.set_yscale("log")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(9, 6.5))
period_boxes(axes[0, 0], pred, "code_Coutry_Region", "log_Light_ppix_Region", "Log light per pixel")
period_boxes(axes[0, 1], pred, "code_Coutry_Region", "GDP_pc_Region", "Observed GDP p.c. (log)", logy=True)
period_boxes(axes[1, 0], t2, None, "pred_GDP_pc_Region", "Predicted GDP p.c. (log)", logy=True)
period_boxes(axes[1, 1], pred, "code_Coutry_Region", "Pop_Region", "Population (log)", logy=True)
fig.suptitle("Region-level variables over time", fontweight="bold"); fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()

cc = [(t3, "GDP_pc_Country", "GDP p.c. (log)", True),
      (t3, "GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "Regional Gini", False),
      (t4, "Resources_rents_share_of_GDP", "Resource rents (% GDP)", False),
      (t4, "Trade_GDP_share", "Trade / GDP", False),
      (t4, "price_gasoline", "Gasoline price ($/L)", False),
      (f5, "Giniall", "Personal income Gini", False)]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(12, 6.5))
for ax, (df, col, title, logy) in zip(axes.flat, cc):
    period_boxes(ax, df, "Country_ISO", col, title, logy=logy)
fig.suptitle("Country-level variables over time", fontweight="bold"); fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Cross-country dynamics of inequality

Before modelling anything, we map the landscape: how the regional Gini differs across world
regions and how the five inequality indices co-move.

```{python}
#| label: eda-region
IDX = ["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "COVW_pred_GDP_pc", "GE_1W_pred_GDP_pc",
       "GE_0W_pred_GDP_pc", "GE_m1W_pred_GDP_pc"]
eda = t3.copy()
eda["wb_group"] = eda["Country_ISO"].map(country_group)
order = eda.groupby("wb_group")["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"].median().sort_values().index.tolist()
data_by = [eda.loc[eda.wb_group == g, "GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"].dropna() for g in order]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 4.4))
bp = ax.boxplot(data_by, vert=False, patch_artist=True, widths=0.6, showfliers=False)
for p in bp["boxes"]:
    p.set(facecolor=STEEL, alpha=0.65, edgecolor=INK)
for m in bp["medians"]:
    m.set(color=ORANGE, linewidth=2)
ax.set_yticklabels(order)
ax.set(xlabel="regional inequality (GINIW)",
       title="Regional inequality across World Bank regions")
plt.show()
print(eda.groupby("wb_group")["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"].median().sort_values().round(4))
```

Sub-Saharan Africa has the highest median regional inequality (about 0.096), two and a half
times North America's (0.038). Rich regions are not only richer; their internal income map is
far more even.

```{python}
#| label: eda-heatmap
cmat = t3[IDX].corr()
print("corr(Gini, CV)   =", round(cmat.iloc[0, 1], 3))
print("corr(Gini, Theil)=", round(cmat.iloc[0, 2], 3))
```

All five indices correlate above 0.9, so the headline Gini results below do not hinge on the
choice of index.

## Predicting GDP from nighttime lights (Table 1)

We regress log regional GDP per capita on log light per pixel plus controls. PyFixest gives
the fixed-effects elasticities; the paper used random effects, which we add as a sidebar.

$$y_r = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \ell_r + \beta_2 g_c + \gamma' X_r + \mu_g + \tau_s + \varepsilon_r$$

```{python}
#| label: table1-fe
GEO = ("log_N_pix_top_cod_1_ppix + log_N_pix_low_cod_1_ppix + log_area + "
       "log_region + log_region_X_log_area")
specs = {
 1: "log_GDP_pc_Region ~ log_Light_ppix_Region",
 2: "log_GDP_pc_Region ~ log_Light_ppix_Region | code_Coutry_Region+satyear",
 4: "log_GDP_pc_Region ~ log_Light_ppix_Region + log_GDP_pc_Country | Country_ISO+satyear",
 7: f"log_GDP_pc_Region ~ log_Light_ppix_Region + log_GDP_pc_Country + {GEO} | group_id+satyear",
}
fe_models = {k: pf.feols(fml, data=pred, vcov={"CRV1": "Country_ISO"})
             for k, fml in specs.items()}
mt.ETable(list(fe_models.values()), model_heads=[f"({k})" for k in specs],
          felabels={"code_Coutry_Region": "Region FE", "Country_ISO": "Country FE",
                    "group_id": "WB-group FE", "satyear": "Satellite FE"},
          coef_fmt="b:.3f* (se:.3f)", show_fe=True).make("gt")
```

Column 2 (region fixed effects) gives the clean within elasticity of **0.190**. Now the
random-effects sidebar that reproduces the paper's preferred column 7.

```{python}
#| label: table1-re
panel    = pred.set_index(["code_Coutry_Region", "year"])
clusters = pd.DataFrame({"c": pd.Categorical(panel["Country_ISO"]).codes}, index=panel.index)
gdum = pd.get_dummies(panel["group_id"], drop_first=True).astype(float)
satm = panel[[f"satyear_{i}" for i in range(1, 8)]].astype(float)
geo  = panel[["log_N_pix_top_cod_1_ppix", "log_N_pix_low_cod_1_ppix",
              "log_area", "log_region", "log_region_X_log_area"]]
X7 = pd.concat([pd.Series(1.0, index=panel.index, name="const"),
                panel[["log_Light_ppix_Region", "log_GDP_pc_Country"]], geo,
                gdum, satm], axis=1)
re7 = RandomEffects(panel["log_GDP_pc_Region"], X7).fit(
    cov_type="clustered", clusters=clusters)
print("RE col 7 light elasticity     =", round(re7.params["log_Light_ppix_Region"], 3))
print("RE col 7 national-GDP elasticity =", round(re7.params["log_GDP_pc_Country"], 3))
```

The random-effects light elasticity is **0.102** (vs 0.049 with fixed effects): random effects
keep the between-region variation the within estimator discards. National income enters at
0.889 — regional income tracks national income almost one-for-one.

```{python}
#| label: prediction
fitted_log = X7.values @ re7.params.reindex(X7.columns).values
obs_log = panel["log_GDP_pc_Region"].values
r = np.corrcoef(fitted_log, obs_log)[0, 1]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 5))
ax.scatter(fitted_log, obs_log, s=8, facecolors="none", edgecolors=STEEL, alpha=0.4)
ax.plot([4, 13], [4, 13], color=ORANGE, lw=2)
ax.set(xlim=(4, 13), ylim=(4, 13), xlabel="predicted log GDP p.c.",
       ylabel="observed log GDP p.c.", title=f"Predicted vs observed (r = {r:.3f})")
plt.show()
print("corr(predicted, observed) =", round(r, 3))
```

Predicted and observed income correlate **0.925** — the calibration generalises across the
whole income range, which licenses predicting income for regions with no statistics.

## Constructing the inequality indicators (Table 2)

Every index starts from population-weighted incomes. The Gini is the average absolute income
gap between two random people; the generalized-entropy indices and the CV weight the tails
differently.

```{python}
#| label: ineq-fn
def ineq_indices(y, w):
    y, w = np.asarray(y, float), np.asarray(w, float)
    ok = np.isfinite(y) & np.isfinite(w) & (w > 0) & (y > 0)
    y, w = y[ok], w[ok]
    sw = w.sum(); mu = (w * y).sum() / sw
    p = w / sw; r = y / mu
    ge_m1 = 0.5 * ((p * r**-1).sum() - 1)
    ge_0  = (p * (-np.log(r))).sum()
    ge_1  = (p * r * np.log(r)).sum()
    cv    = np.sqrt(2 * 0.5 * ((p * r**2).sum() - 1))
    gini  = (np.abs(y[:, None] - y[None, :]) * np.outer(w, w)).sum() / (2 * sw**2 * mu)
    return dict(GINIW=gini, GE_m1W=ge_m1, GE_0W=ge_0, GE_1W=ge_1, COVW=cv)

deu = t2[(t2.Country_ISO == "DEU") & (t2.year == 2010)]
print("Germany 2010 (", len(deu), "regions):",
      {k: round(v, 4) for k, v in ineq_indices(deu["pred_GDP_pc_Region"], deu["Pop_Region"]).items()})
```

Germany's 16 regions yield a low population-weighted Gini of 0.028 — exactly what we expect for
a country whose regions cluster near the average. Now the role of population weights.

```{python}
#| label: pop-weights
def gini_unweighted(y):
    y = np.asarray(y, float); y = y[np.isfinite(y) & (y > 0)]
    n, mu = y.size, y.mean()
    return np.abs(y[:, None] - y[None, :]).sum() / (2 * n**2 * mu)

rows = []
for (iso, yr_), g in t2.groupby(["Country_ISO", "year"]):
    v = ineq_indices(g["pred_GDP_pc_Region"], g["Pop_Region"])
    v.update(Country_ISO=iso, year=yr_, GINI_unw=gini_unweighted(g["pred_GDP_pc_Region"]))
    rows.append(v)
built = pd.DataFrame(rows)
w = built.dropna(subset=["GINIW", "GINI_unw"])
print("corr(weighted, unweighted) =", round(w["GINIW"].corr(w["GINI_unw"]), 3))
print("mean(weighted - unweighted) =", round((w["GINIW"] - w["GINI_unw"]).mean(), 4))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 5))
ax.scatter(w["GINI_unw"], w["GINIW"], s=10, facecolors="none", edgecolors=STEEL, alpha=0.45)
mx = max(w["GINI_unw"].max(), w["GINIW"].max()) * 1.02
ax.plot([0, mx], [0, mx], color=ORANGE, lw=2)
ax.set(xlabel="unweighted Gini", ylabel="population-weighted Gini",
       title="The role of population weights")
plt.show()
```

Weighted and unweighted Gini correlate only **0.75**, and weighting *lowers* inequality on
average: small income-extreme regions count for less when we weight by people. Always report
your weighting.

## The regional Kuznets curve (Table 3)

```{python}
#| label: kuznets
def collapse5(df, vars_):
    d = df[(df.year >= 1990) & (df.year <= 2014)].copy()
    d["p5"] = pd.cut(d.year, [1989, 1994, 1999, 2004, 2009, 2014],
                     labels=[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]).astype("int64")
    return d.groupby(["Country_ISO", "p5"], as_index=False)[vars_].mean()

agg = collapse5(t3, ["GDP_pc_Country"] + IDX)
agg["lg"] = np.log(agg["GDP_pc_Country"]); agg["lg2"] = agg.lg**2; agg["lg3"] = agg.lg**3
def kuz_fit(dv, rhs):
    return pf.feols(f"{dv} ~ {rhs} | Country_ISO+p5", data=agg, vcov={"CRV1": "Country_ISO"})
k1 = kuz_fit("GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "lg")
k2 = kuz_fit("GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "lg + lg2")
k3 = kuz_fit("GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "lg + lg2 + lg3")
k_other = {c: kuz_fit(c, "lg + lg2 + lg3") for c in IDX[1:]}
mt.ETable([k1, k2, k3] + [k_other[c] for c in IDX[1:]],
          model_heads=["(1) Gini, linear", "(2) Gini, quadratic", "(3) Gini, cubic",
                       "(4) CV", "(5) Theil", "(6) MLD", "(7) GE(-1)"],
          labels={"lg": "log GDP p.c.", "lg2": "(log GDP p.c.)^2", "lg3": "(log GDP p.c.)^3"},
          felabels={"Country_ISO": "Country FE", "p5": "Period FE"},
          coef_fmt="b:.3f* (se:.3f)", show_fe=True).make("gt")
```

The cubic is **0.293 / −0.032 / 0.001** — positive, negative, positive: an N-shape. Inequality
rises with development, falls, then edges up at the very top.

```{python}
#| label: kuznets-fig
mfe = smf.ols("GINIW_pred_GDP_pc ~ lg + lg2 + lg3 + C(Country_ISO) + C(p5)", agg).fit()
bb = {k: mfe.params[k] for k in ["lg", "lg2", "lg3"]}
peff = {1: 0.0}
for k in (2, 3, 4, 5):
    peff[k] = mfe.params.get(f"C(p5)[T.{k}]", 0.0)
agg["partial"] = agg["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"] - agg["p5"].map(peff)
cons = (agg["partial"] - (bb["lg"]*agg.lg + bb["lg2"]*agg.lg2 + bb["lg3"]*agg.lg3)).mean()
xs = np.linspace(5.5, 11.8, 200)
ys = cons + bb["lg"]*xs + bb["lg2"]*xs**2 + bb["lg3"]*xs**3
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.5))
ax.scatter(agg.lg, agg.partial, s=12, facecolors="none", edgecolors=STEEL, alpha=0.5)
ax.plot(xs, ys, color=INK, lw=2.4)
ax.set(xlim=(5.5, 11.8), ylim=(0, 0.16), xlabel="log GDP per capita",
       ylabel="partial regional inequality (GINIW)", title="The regional Kuznets curve")
plt.show()
```

## Turning points and the discriminant test

A cubic *can* bend twice — but does it, and inside the income range we observe? The marginal
effect $\partial\,\mathrm{GINIW}/\partial \ln Y = \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 \ln Y + 3\beta_3 (\ln Y)^2$
is zero at the turning points, and two **real** turning points exist only when the discriminant
$D = \beta_2^2 - 3\beta_1\beta_3$ is positive. (Significance of all three terms is necessary but
not sufficient: we also need $D>0$ **and** both turning points inside the observed range.) The
same two checks are developed at length in the R companion post
[r_kuznets](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/r_kuznets/).

```{python}
#| label: turning-points
GREY = "#b0a89a"
b1, b2, b3c = [k3.coef()[t] for t in ["lg", "lg2", "lg3"]]
D = b2**2 - 3*b1*b3c
roots = np.sort([(-b2 - np.sqrt(D))/(3*b3c), (-b2 + np.sqrt(D))/(3*b3c)])
lo, hi = agg.GDP_pc_Country.min(), agg.GDP_pc_Country.max()
print(f"D = {D:+.6f}  (>0 -> two turning points)")
print("turning points $:", np.exp(roots).round(0),
      "| income range $:", round(lo), "-", round(hi))

xg = np.linspace(agg.lg.min(), agg.lg.max(), 200)
deriv = b1 + 2*b2*xg + 3*b3c*xg**2
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.2))
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.9); ax.plot(xg, deriv, color=STEEL, lw=2.2)
for r, u in zip(roots, np.exp(roots)):
    ax.axvline(r, color=ORANGE, ls="--", lw=1.4); ax.plot([r], [0], "o", color=TEAL, ms=8)
    ax.annotate(f"${u:,.0f}", (r, 0), (r, deriv.max()*0.5), ha="center", color=TEAL)
ax.set(xlabel="log GDP per capita", ylabel="marginal effect",
       title="Where does the curve turn?")
plt.show()
```

Both turning points (about \$2,287 and \$77,206) fall inside the observed range, so this is a
genuine — if marginal — N-shape: $D = +0.000035$ sits just above the $D=0$ knife-edge. Holding
the linear and cubic terms fixed and varying **only** the squared term shows how the sign of $D$
decides the shape:

```{python}
#| label: discriminant-regimes
b2_zero = -np.sqrt(3*b1*b3c)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.4))
for ax, (lab, p2) in zip(axes, [("D < 0 (monotonic)", -0.025),
                                ("D = 0 (inflection)", b2_zero),
                                ("D > 0 (genuine N)", b2)]):
    f = b1*xg + p2*xg**2 + b3c*xg**3; f = f - f.mean()
    ax.plot(xg, f, color=ORANGE, lw=2.0); ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.7)
    ax.set_title(f"{lab}\nD = {p2**2 - 3*b1*b3c:+.5f}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("log GDP per capita")
fig.suptitle("Same significant terms, three shapes", fontweight="bold")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Significance is not shape.** A cubic can have all three terms significant yet be monotonic
($D<0$) or turn only outside the data ($D>0$ with a turning point at an absurd income). Always
report the coefficients, compute $D$, and confirm the turning points are in range before claiming
an N-shape — see
[r_kuznets](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/r_kuznets/#7-turning-points-and-the-discriminant-test)
for the full treatment, including the Bayesian model-averaging analogue.

## Determinants of regional inequality (Table 4)

```{python}
#| label: determinants
agg4 = collapse5(t4, ["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "GDP_pc_Country", "Pop_Country",
                      "Resources_rents_share_of_GDP", "Arable_land", "Trade_GDP_share",
                      "FDI_share_of_GDP", "area", "price_gasoline", "Aid",
                      "School_enrollment_secondary", "GINIW_Eth_light", "Polity2", "fedelupd2"])
agg4["lg"] = np.log(agg4["GDP_pc_Country"]); agg4["lg2"] = agg4.lg**2; agg4["lg3"] = agg4.lg**3
agg4["aid_GDP"] = agg4["Aid"] / (agg4["GDP_pc_Country"] * agg4["Pop_Country"])
agg4["Resources_rents_share_of_GDP"] /= 100
agg4["School_enrollment_secondary"] /= 100
agg4["Area_X_price_gasoline"] = agg4["area"] * agg4["price_gasoline"] / 1e7
agg4["lgXfed"] = agg4["lg"] * agg4["fedelupd2"]
def det_fit(extra):
    return pf.feols(f"GINIW_pred_GDP_pc ~ lg+lg2+lg3 + {extra} | Country_ISO+p5",
                    data=agg4, vcov={"CRV1": "Country_ISO"})
d0 = pf.feols("GINIW_pred_GDP_pc ~ lg+lg2+lg3 | Country_ISO+p5",
              data=agg4, vcov={"CRV1": "Country_ISO"})
d1 = det_fit("Resources_rents_share_of_GDP + Arable_land")
d2 = det_fit("Trade_GDP_share + FDI_share_of_GDP")
d3 = det_fit("price_gasoline + Area_X_price_gasoline")
d_inst = det_fit("Polity2 + lgXfed")            # institutions (ICRG omitted)
d4 = det_fit("aid_GDP + School_enrollment_secondary")
d5 = det_fit("GINIW_Eth_light")
mt.ETable([d0, d1, d2, d3, d_inst, d4, d5],
          model_heads=["(0) baseline", "(1) resources", "(2) openness", "(3) mobility",
                       "(4) institutions", "(5) transfers/edu", "(6) ethnicity"],
          labels={"lg": "log GDP p.c.", "lg2": "(log GDP p.c.)^2", "lg3": "(log GDP p.c.)^3",
                  "Resources_rents_share_of_GDP": "Resource rents/GDP", "Arable_land": "Arable land",
                  "Trade_GDP_share": "Trade/GDP", "FDI_share_of_GDP": "FDI/GDP",
                  "price_gasoline": "Gasoline price", "Area_X_price_gasoline": "Area x gasoline",
                  "Polity2": "Polity2", "lgXfed": "log GDP x Federal", "aid_GDP": "Aid/GDP",
                  "School_enrollment_secondary": "Schooling", "GINIW_Eth_light": "Ethnic inequality"},
          felabels={"Country_ISO": "Country FE", "p5": "Period FE"},
          coef_fmt="b:.3f* (se:.3f)", show_fe=True).make("gt")
```

The strongest correlate is **ethnic inequality (0.071)**. Resource rents push inequality up
(0.018); a larger arable-land share pulls it down (−0.053).

## Spatial robustness: Conley standard errors (Table B.4)

```{python}
#| label: conley
tb4["satyear"] = sum(i * tb4[f"satyear_{i}"] for i in range(1, 8)).astype(int)
cols = ["log_GDP_pc_Region", "log_Light_ppix_Region", "Latitude", "Longitude",
        "code_Coutry_Region", "satyear"]
dfb = tb4.dropna(subset=cols).copy()
cnt = dfb["code_Coutry_Region"].value_counts()
dfb = dfb[dfb["code_Coutry_Region"].isin(cnt[cnt > 1].index)].copy()
mb = pf.feols("log_GDP_pc_Region ~ log_Light_ppix_Region | code_Coutry_Region + satyear",
              data=dfb)
b_b4 = float(mb.coef()["log_Light_ppix_Region"])
xfit = pf.feols("log_Light_ppix_Region ~ 1 | code_Coutry_Region + satyear", data=dfb)
xt, u = np.asarray(xfit.resid()), np.asarray(mb.resid())
sxx = float(np.sum(xt**2))
gb = dfb.assign(xu=xt*u).groupby("code_Coutry_Region")
agg_s = gb["xu"].sum().to_numpy()
lat = np.radians(gb["Latitude"].first().to_numpy())
lon = np.radians(gb["Longitude"].first().to_numpy())
R, nr = 6371.0, agg_s.size
def conley_var(dist):
    v = 0.0
    for i in range(nr):
        a = (np.sin((lat-lat[i])/2)**2 + np.cos(lat[i])*np.cos(lat)*np.sin((lon-lon[i])/2)**2)
        dij = 2*R*np.arcsin(np.minimum(1.0, np.sqrt(a)))
        v += agg_s[i] * np.sum(np.maximum(0.0, 1 - dij/dist) * agg_s)
    return v / sxx**2
print("point estimate =", round(b_b4, 3))
for r_ in (1000, 2500, 5000):
    print(f"Conley SE @ {r_} km = {np.sqrt(conley_var(r_)):.3f}")
print("naive iid SE     =", round(float(mb.se()['log_Light_ppix_Region']), 3))
```

Allowing for spatial correlation roughly doubles to triples the standard error (0.013 →
0.026–0.037), but the elasticity of 0.190 stays far from zero.

## Regional versus personal inequality (Figure 5a)

```{python}
#| label: fig5
d5f = f5[(f5.year > 2000) & (f5.year < 2013)].copy()
a5 = d5f.groupby("Country_ISO", as_index=False)[["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc", "Giniall"]].mean()
a5["GINIall_100"] = a5["Giniall"] / 100
p5 = a5.dropna(subset=["GINIall_100", "GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"])
slope, icept = np.polyfit(p5["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"], p5["GINIall_100"], 1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 4.6))
ax.scatter(p5["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"], p5["GINIall_100"], marker="^", s=24,
           facecolors="none", edgecolors=STEEL)
xs = np.linspace(p5["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"].min(), p5["GINIW_pred_GDP_pc"].max(), 200)
ax.plot(xs, slope*xs + icept, color=ORANGE, lw=2.2)
ax.set(xlabel="interregional inequality (GINIW)", ylabel="interpersonal inequality (Gini)",
       title=f"Regional vs personal inequality (slope {slope:.3f})")
plt.show()
print("n =", len(p5), "countries | slope =", round(slope, 3))
```

Across 144 countries the household-income Gini rises with the regional Gini (slope **0.587**):
places with wide gaps between regions also have wide gaps between people.

## References

1. Lessmann, C., & Seidel, A. (2017). Regional inequality, convergence, and its determinants —
   A view from outer space. *European Economic Review*, 92, 110–132.
2. Henderson, J. V., Storeygard, A., & Weil, D. N. (2012). Measuring economic growth from outer
   space. *American Economic Review*, 102(2), 994–1028.
3. Gennaioli, N., La Porta, R., Lopez-de-Silanes, F., & Shleifer, A. (2014). Growth in regions.
   *Journal of Economic Growth*, 19(3), 259–309.
4. [PyFixest documentation](https://py-econometrics.github.io/pyfixest/) ·
   [linearmodels documentation](https://bashtage.github.io/linearmodels/)
